choices.prompt: According to the module's testing pyramid, which test layer belongs at the wide base — fast, isolated, with no network or database — and should run as the first job in the pipeline?
choices: Unit tests, because thousands run in seconds so a broken unit fails in under a minute
Integration tests, because they verify components actually talk to each other
Load tests, because they validate behavior under realistic traffic
End-to-end tests, because they exercise the whole system the way a user would
correct_answers: Unit tests, because thousands run in seconds so a broken unit fails in under a minute

choices.prompt: A pull request raises project coverage from 72% to 91%, but a reviewer is uneasy. Based on the module, what is the most likely reason coverage can rise while real safety does not?
choices: Coverage measures lines executed, not assertions made, so the new tests may run code without meaningfully verifying it
Coverage only counts branches, so adding line-based tests inflates the number without testing anything
Coverage tools cannot measure integration tests, so the figure reflects only unit tests
Coverage above 90% is always inaccurate because the Cobertura format rounds up
correct_answers: Coverage measures lines executed, not assertions made, so the new tests may run code without meaningfully verifying it

choices.prompt: In the module's distinction between the two gating mechanisms, which statements are TRUE? Select all that apply.
choices: A quality gate is an automatic pass/fail condition inside the pipeline, such as tests green or coverage above threshold
A release gate sits between stages and guards promotion, for example between deploy-to-staging and deploy-to-production
Release gates come in two flavors: approvals (a named human or group signs off) and automatic gates (the pipeline polls an external signal)
A passing test suite (quality gate) is what decides a change should ship right now
correct_answers: A quality gate is an automatic pass/fail condition inside the pipeline, such as tests green or coverage above threshold
A release gate sits between stages and guards promotion, for example between deploy-to-staging and deploy-to-production
Release gates come in two flavors: approvals (a named human or group signs off) and automatic gates (the pipeline polls an external signal)

choices.prompt: In the order-service walkthrough YAML, which task enforces the quality gate by failing the build when line coverage falls below the 80% floor?
choices: BuildQualityChecks@9 with checkCoverage: true, coverageType: 'lines', coverageThreshold: '80'
PublishCodeCoverageResults@2 with the Cobertura summaryFileLocation
UseDotNet@2 configured with packageType: sdk, version: '8.x'
The dotnet test script using --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage"
correct_answers: BuildQualityChecks@9 with checkCoverage: true, coverageType: 'lines', coverageThreshold: '80'

choices.prompt: A build has green tests, 85% coverage, and a clean security scan, but the on-call engineer wants to hold it until Monday rather than deploy on a Friday afternoon. Per the module, which mechanism enforces that decision and what is it?
choices: An environment approval acting as a release gate — the build is good, but a human controls when it deploys
A coverage threshold acting as a quality gate — it blocks the build until conditions are met
A linting check acting as a quality gate — it refuses to promote until the code is clean
An integration-test stage acting as a release gate — it verifies dependencies before production
correct_answers: An environment approval acting as a release gate — the build is good, but a human controls when it deploys

choices.prompt: Per the module, why are integration tests placed later in the pipeline than unit tests rather than running first?
choices: They are slower and need provisioned dependencies, so they run after units pass
They are faster than unit tests but require a clean security scan to begin
They must run on a schedule rather than on every commit because they are the most expensive layer
They can only run on the developer's machine, so the pipeline defers them to a later stage
correct_answers: They are slower and need provisioned dependencies, so they run after units pass

choices.prompt: The module describes automatic release gates as polling an external signal and proceeding only when it is good. Which of the following are given as examples of such signals? Select all that apply.
choices: A monitoring system reporting healthy error rates
A change-management ticket marked approved
A security scan with no open findings
A unit-test job finishing in under a minute
correct_answers: A monitoring system reporting healthy error rates
A security scan with no open findings

choices.prompt: The module says pointing integration tests at a long-lived shared database is a pitfall. What problem does it cause, and what does the module recommend instead?
choices: It makes tests order-dependent and flaky; spin up disposable dependencies per run (e.g. containers) so each run starts clean
It inflates coverage numbers; gate on changed-code coverage rather than total coverage
It slows the unit layer; move most test mass to the base of the pyramid
It bypasses the release gate; wire change approval as a release gate before production
correct_answers: It makes tests order-dependent and flaky; spin up disposable dependencies per run (e.g. containers) so each run starts clean

choices.prompt: According to the module's economic reasoning for the pyramid, which statements are TRUE about the cost of catching a bug at different layers? Select all that apply.
choices: A bug caught by a unit test costs seconds
The same bug caught by a load test costs a provisioned environment and a long run
The same bug caught in production costs an incident
A bug caught by a unit test costs a provisioned environment, which is why units run last
correct_answers: A bug caught by a unit test costs seconds
The same bug caught by a load test costs a provisioned environment and a long run
The same bug caught in production costs an incident

choices.prompt: The module advises treating coverage as a 'floor and a trend.' Which practice best reflects that advice?
choices: Fail the build if coverage on changed code drops below a threshold and watch the direction over time, without making 'raise the number' the goal
Set a single fixed target percentage for the whole repository and reward teams that exceed it
Measure coverage only on load tests, since they exercise the most realistic paths
Disable the build when coverage is below 100%, since any untested line is unsafe to ship
correct_answers: Fail the build if coverage on changed code drops below a threshold and watch the direction over time, without making 'raise the number' the goal

llm.prompt: Explain why the testing pyramid is shaped like a pyramid rather than a rectangle, and what goes wrong when a team inverts it. Reference the layers and the economic reasoning the module gives.
llm.reference_answer: The shape is economic: tests trade speed for realism, and the cost of catching a bug rises sharply the later it is caught. A bug caught by a unit test costs seconds, the same bug caught by a load test costs a provisioned environment and a long run, and caught in production it costs an incident — so you push detection down and left. The wide base is fast, isolated unit tests (no network or database) that run thousands in seconds and run first in the pipeline; above them are slower integration tests needing provisioned dependencies; the narrow top is expensive load tests run least often, on a schedule or before a major release. A team that inverts the pyramid — mostly slow end-to-end tests with few unit tests — gets a pipeline that is slow, flaky, and expensive, so developers learn to ignore red runs and stop trusting it.

llm.prompt: Compare quality gates and release gates: how do they differ in mechanism and purpose, and why does the module warn against confusing them? Use the order-service example to illustrate.
llm.reference_answer: A quality gate is an automatic, pass/fail condition inside the pipeline that a build must satisfy to continue — tests green, coverage above threshold, no high-severity vulnerabilities, linting clean — and it keeps bad builds from advancing. A release gate sits between stages and guards promotion (for example between staging and production); it comes as approvals, where a named human or group signs off for governance/change control, or as automatic gates, where the pipeline polls an external signal like a monitoring system, a change-management ticket, or a security scan and proceeds only when the signal is good. The module warns against confusing them because a passing test suite does not mean a change should ship now, and an approval does not mean the code is correct — quality gates govern correctness, release gates govern timing and authority. In the order-service pipeline the BuildQualityChecks coverage check is the quality gate that would have caught the untested pricing refactor, while the production environment's approval is the release gate where a human decides when the good build goes out.

llm.prompt: Walk through how you would treat code coverage in the pipeline so it helps rather than misleads, including the specific pitfall to avoid and the supporting practice the module recommends.
llm.reference_answer: Code coverage measures which lines or branches your tests execute, so integrated into the pipeline it answers 'is this code path tested at all?' — excellent for catching a change like the pricing refactor that shipped with zero tests touching the new branch. But coverage measures execution, not assertion: a test can run a line and assert nothing meaningful, so ninety percent coverage with weak assertions catches less than sixty percent with sharp ones. I would treat coverage as a floor and a trend — fail the build if coverage on changed code drops below a threshold (the walkthrough gates at 80% line coverage via BuildQualityChecks) and watch the direction over time — and never make 'raise the number' the goal, since the cheapest way to raise it is assertion-free tests. The supporting practice is to review assertion quality in code review so the metric reflects real verification rather than just executed lines.
